coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen and copper (II) co-doped carbon dots for applications in ascorbic acid determination by non-oxidation reduction strategy and cellular imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yushan
Wu, Peng
Wu, Xueyun
Ma, Chunhui
Luo, Sha
Xu, Mingcong
Li, Wei
Liu, Shouxin
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen and copper co-doped carbon dots (Cu-NCDs) were prepared by solvothermal carbonization of folic acid and CuCl2. The N-containing groups including sp2-hybridized CN, porphyrin C–N–C and amino N in N-(C) 3 or H–N-(C) 2, and the Cu-containing group, N–Cu–N, have formed on the surface and framework of Cu-NCDs. The Cu-NCDs were monodisperse with the average particle diameter of 3.57 nm and exhibited dual fluorescence emission peaks at 410 and 470 nm with the excitation wavelength of 340 nm. The new emission center of 410 nm might originate from the Cu doping which changed the structural network and surface state of the carbon dots. The Cu-NCDs exhibited good fluorescence quenching response towards ascorbic acid (AA) from 0.02 to 40 μM (R2 = 0.992), and the limit of detection was 17.8 nM. The mechanism of the quenching process is non-oxidation reduction strategy based static quenching (SQE). Cu doping can improve the selectivity and sensitivity for Cu-NCDs towards AA benefiting from its chelation effect towards multi-hydroxyl in AA. Cu and N doping cause positively charged surface of Cu-NCDs, improving the interaction towards AA and then the stable Cu-NCD-based non-luminescent compounds formed, which resulted SQE. The Cu-NCDs possessed low cellular toxicity and showed good uptake by HepG2 cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Nitrogen and copper (II) co-doping
Dual-emission
Ascorbic acid detection
Static quenching
Cellular imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst otherkwds primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cu-NCDs preparation and ascorbic acid determination

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the preparation process for Cu-NCDs.
(a) TEM and HRTEM (inset) image of the Cu-NCDs and size distribution histogram of the NCDs (inset). (b) XRD pattern from the Cu-NCDs.
FT-IR spectra of Cu-NCDs and NCDs.
(a) XPS full-scan spectra of the Cu-NCDs. (b) High-resolution spectra of the C1s peaks. (c) High-resolution spectra of the N1s peaks. (d) High-resolution spectra of the Cu2p peaks.

(a) UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence excitation spectrum spectra (fluorescence emission at 470 nm), the inset of a shows a digital photograph of Cu-NCDs under 365 nm UV light. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of Cu-NCDs at different excitation wavelengths. (c) Fluorescence emission spectra of Cu-NCDs at different pH values, inset shows fluorescence emission intensity at different pH values.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of Cu-NCDs in the presence of the increasing concentration of AA (from top to bottom: 0, 0.02, 0.2, 10, 20, 40, 160, 240, 320, 400, 560, 720, 1500 and 2000 μM, under the excitation wavelength of 340 nm). The inset displays the relationship between quenching efficiency and AA concentration. (b) Relationship between fluorescence intensity and AA concentration. (c) Normalized fluorescence intensity of the Cu-NCDs in the presence of a series of metal ions (concentration: 2000 μM). (d) Normalized fluorescence intensity of the Cu-NCDs in the presence of a series of small organic molecules (concentration: 2000 μM).
(a) Fluorescence decay curves of Cu-NCDs and Cu-NCDs/AA mixture. (b) Model of AA detection mechanism.

(a) Cytotoxicity studies of the Cu-NCDs (24 h post-treatment) by the MTT assay for HepG2 cells. (b) Bright-field microscopy image of HepG2 cells (A, A′), fluorescence microscopy images of HepG2 cells incubated with 100 μg mL−1 Cu-NCDs for 6 h (B–D, B′–D′). The fluorescence images from B/B′ to D/D′ were obtained under excitation at 300, 360 and 440 nm, respectively.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen and copper (II) co-doped carbon dots for applications in ascorbic acid determination by non-oxidation reduction strategy and cellular imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science and Technology of Ministry of Education, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin, 150040, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science and Technology of Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science and Technology of Ministry of Education, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin, 150040, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen and copper co-doped carbon dots (Cu-NCDs) were prepared by solvothermal carbonization of folic acid and CuCl2. The N-containing groups including sp2-hybridized CN, porphyrin C–N–C and amino N in N-(C) 3 or H–N-(C) 2, and the Cu-containing group, N–Cu–N, have formed on the surface and framework of Cu-NCDs. The Cu-NCDs were monodisperse with the average particle diameter of 3.57 nm and exhibited dual fluorescence emission peaks at 410 and 470 nm with the excitation wavelength of 340 nm. The new emission center of 410 nm might originate from the Cu doping which changed the structural network and surface state of the carbon dots. The Cu-NCDs exhibited good fluorescence quenching response towards ascorbic acid (AA) from 0.02 to 40 μM (R2 = 0.992), and the limit of detection was 17.8 nM. The mechanism of the quenching process is non-oxidation reduction strategy based static quenching (SQE). Cu doping can improve the selectivity and sensitivity for Cu-NCDs towards AA benefiting from its chelation effect towards multi-hydroxyl in AA. Cu and N doping cause positively charged surface of Cu-NCDs, improving the interaction towards AA and then the stable Cu-NCD-based non-luminescent compounds formed, which resulted SQE. The Cu-NCDs possessed low cellular toxicity and showed good uptake by HepG2 cells.

One-pot solvothermal synthesis of Nitrogen and copper (II) co-doped carbon dots with dual emission peaks for applications in ascorbic acid determination by non-oxidation reduction strategy based static quenching mechanism and cellular imaging.
Image 1

ce:keyword.ce:text: Nitrogen and copper (II) co-doped carbon dots

ce:section.ce:para: Functional modification carbon dots (CDs) have gained extensive attention owing to their novel optical properties different from pure CDs, and potential applications in photocatalysis technology [1,2], analyte detection [3,4], light-emitting devices [5,6], bioimaging, and drug delivery [7,8]. Surface functionalization and heteroatoms doping are effective approaches to modulate CDs. They are relatively convenient for changing the electronic structures associated with the energy band gap of CDs, which endow CDs with different intrinsic properties and make them more suitable for analytical applications such as for pH, solvent, ion, biological and chemical small molecules determination et al. [9–11]. Most of the previous research have used nonmetallic heteroatoms, such as N [12], S [13], B [14], P [15] and Se [16] as dopant. Their similar atomic structures to C atoms can efficiently modulate the physicochemical properties of CDs. However, compared with nonmetallic heteroatoms, metallic heteroatoms especially the transition metal ions have more electrons and unoccupied orbitals, which make it easier to alter the charge density on the surface of CDs [17]. Moreover, the chelation effect for metal ions is beneficial to combine with amino and carboxyl groups in CD via chemical reaction procedure [18]. Recently, researches on CDs doped with metal ions such as Cu, Mn [19], Fe [20], Zn [21], Gd [22] and Co [23], have been reported. Among them, Cu has widely been used in doping CDs owing to its safety and ease-of-interaction with functional groups on CD's surface. Furthermore, it is easier to chelate Cu with most small molecules, which improves the selectivity and sensitivity for CDs towards the analyte in the environment. At present, N acting as a typical dopant is the most popular in the reports on heteroatom-doped CDs, the atomic size and valence electrons structure can be comparable to that of C. N doping was found to be of critical importance to improve the quantum yield (QY) and analytical detection performance for CDs, because of the N doping causing the CDs to have a positively charged surface, which significantly improved the interaction between CDs and the analyte [24].

Therefore, the N and Cu doping in CDs can modify the optical properties and improve the selectivity and sensitivity of CDs towards the analyte. There have already been studies on Cu-NCDs. Meng et al. synthesized active copper-containing carbon dots (Cu-CDs), which processed 4-times higher fluorescence intensity than un-doped CDs. The Cu-CDs can catalyze the oxidation of laccase substrate p-phenylenediamine and detect hydroquinone based on laccase-mimicking and fluorescence quenching [25]. Hu et al. synthesized Cu-NCDs by the hydrothermal carbonization of citric acid, copper acetate and diethylenetriamine. The Cu-NCDs showed high optical response ability towards H2O2 from the inner filter effect (IFE) between Cu-NCDs and DAP (2, 3-diaminophenazine, the production for o-phenylenediamine oxidation). The Cu-NCDs can be used as ratiometric fluorescence determination platform for cholesterol and xanthine, whose metabolize can produce H2O2 26. Apart from the application in the field of analytical determination, Cu-NCDs have also been used as photocatalytic materials. The doping of Cu can increase the density of the catalytically active centers of Cu-NCDs using for combining redox species in catalytic reactions via influencing the spin density and the charge distribution of Cu-NCDs. Zeng et al. reported Cu-NCDs for the photocatalytic degradation of 1, 4-dihydro-2,6-dimethylpyridine-3, 5-dicarboxylate (1,4-DHP). N and Cu co-doping effectively improves the electron accepting/donating abilities and electric conductivity, and the photocatalytic efficiency is also improved 3.5-times after doping [27]. Moreover, bioimaging is one of the most significant applications for CDs owning to their excellent fluorescence and high biocompatibility. However, some metal-doped CDs are detrimental to cells and organism in high concentrations, and therefore the development of metal-doped CDs with low cytotoxicity is necessary.

AA is an essential vitamin that is required to maintain a healthy physiological function, and its deficiency directly leads to diseases like cancer, cardiovascular disease and Parkinson's disease [28]. The determination of AA is meaningful, CDs possess great application prospect in AA determination among numerous fluorescent probes [29]. Wu et al. reported a facile NCDs-based fluorescent sensor for AA in biological fluids by the IFE and the SQE rather than an oxidation reduction strategy [30]. Apart from this process, currently, most of the reported CD-based AA sensors have indirectly incorporated the oxidation reduction strategy between the metal ions and AA [31–33] or passivation with organic molecules or compositing with other materials. Therefore, a CD-based AA fluorescent probe without passivation by organic molecules or compositing with other materials that results from a non-oxidation reduction strategy is meaningful. Moreover, a direct AA determination process is necessary, because it can simplify the operation and reduce the interference from impurities.

Herein, a solvothermal carbonization for the synthesis of Cu-NCDs with dual emission peaks was reported. The Cu-NCDs exhibited excitation wavelength- and pH-dependent fluorescence property. Based on the characterization results, the related mechanism was elucidated. The Cu-NCDs were used as fluorescent probe for AA determination and the application for HepG2 cellular imaging was also reported.

Folic acid (FA), AA, cysteine, histidine, phenylalanine, tyrosine, tryptophan, glutamic acid, lysine, valine, glutathione, vitamin B12, vitamin B3, citric acid, malic acid, tartaric acid, glucose and dopamine were purchased from Lanji Technology Development Ltd., Shanghai, China. Pb(NO3)2, HgCl2, CrCl3, FeCl2, CuCl, CuCl2, CoCl2, CaCl2, MgCl2, Cd(NO3)2, MnCl2, Ni(NO3)2, SnCl2, Al2(SO4)3 were purchased from Kermel Chemical Reagent Ltd., Tianjin, China. Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was purchased from Guangfu Technology Development Ltd., Tianjin, China. Fetal bovine serum (FBS), 3-(4, 5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2, 5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), trypsinase and Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM, High glucose) were purchased from Solarbio Science and Technology Ltd., Beijing, China. All chemical reagents were of analytical grade and were used as received. Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, pH = 7.4, 10 mM) was used to maintain the pH environment in the AA determination process.

(High-resolution) transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM/TEM) images were obtained by a JEM 2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL Ltd., Japan) with an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. X-ray diffraction measurements (XRD) tests were collected on a D8 Advance instrument (Bruker AXS, Germany) with Cu Kα radiation. The apparent zeta potential was measured by dynamic light scattering (DLS) on a Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using an Escalab 250 XI spectrometer (Thermo, USA) with Al-Kα radiation (hυ = 1486.6 eV). Binding energies were referenced to the C1s line at 284.8 eV. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra based on infrared transmittance measurements were measured on a Nicolet iS10 FT-IR spectrometer (Nicolet, USA) from 400 to 4000 cm−1 with a resolution of 4 cm−1. UV–Vis spectra were acquired by a TU-1950 UV–Vis spectrometer. The fluorescence study was conducted with an Agilent Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA) equipped with a 150 W xenon lamp as the excitation source. Fluorescence lifetime measurements were recorded using a FluoroLog-3 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Jobin Yvon Inc., USA), with excitation/emission wavelengths of 340/470 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cu-NCDs preparation and ascorbic acid determination
ce:section.ce:para: The Cu-NCDs were prepared by solvothermal carbonization of FA and CuCl2 in ethanol (Fig. 1 ). 0.05 g of FA and 0.12 g of CuCl2 were dissolved in 15 mL of ethanol. The solution was transferred into a 50 mL of polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 6 h. The crude product was filtered through a nylon filtration membrane filter (0.22 μm) and removed the ethanol by rotary evaporation. Adding 20 mL of deionized water into above solid product and subjected to dialysis in water (1000 Da molecular weight cut-off) for approximately 24, 36 and 48 h. The results indicated there is no very obvious effect on the fluorescence intensity for dialysis time, therefore, the 24 h was chosen as the optimal dialysis time (Fig. S1). The resulting CDs are referred as Cu-NCDs. CDs were also prepared through a similar procedure with only FA (i.e., no CuCl2) as comparison, and are referred as NCDs.

The AA determination was performed in PBS solution. Different concentrations of AA were added into the Cu-NCD PBS solution. The final concentration of AA ranged from 0 to 2 mM. The solutions were reacted for 30 min before fluorescence measurement. All samples were excited at 340 nm, and the fluorescent emission were collected at the wavelength of 470 nm. To explore the selectivity and sensitivity of detection for AA, diverse of metal ions including Pb2+, Hg2+, Cr3+, Fe2+, Cu+, Cu2+，Co2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Sn2+, Al3+ and small organic molecules including cysteine, histidine, phenylalanine, tyrosine, tryptophan, glutamic acid, lysine, valine, glutathione, glucose and dopamine were explored in place of AA. For comparison, the detection of AA was also performed in a NCDs PBS solution with the same determination conditions.

The cellular toxicity was tested according to the previous work. The HepG2 cells were cultured in 96-well plates first for 24 h in an incubator (37 °C, 5% CO2), and for another 24 h after the culture medium was replaced by 200 μL of fresh DMEM, containing the Cu-NCDs with different concentrations (0, 25, 50, 100, 200, 400, 600 800, 1000, 1200 μg mL−1). Then, 20 μL of 5 mg mL−1 MTT solution was added to each cell well. The cells were further incubated for 4 h, followed by removing the DMEM with MTT, and then 150 μL of DMSO was added. Subsequently, the absorbance of the optical density was measured with an automatic ELISA analyzer (MD Spectra Max M3, USA). A control experiment regardless of Cu-NCDs was made as well. Each experiment was conducted three times, and the data were averaged. The cell viability was estimated according to the following equation:
Cell Viability (%) = OD Treated/OD Control

Where (optical density) ODControl was obtained in the absence of Cu-NCDs, and OD Treated was obtained in the presence of Cu-NCDs. When the cell viability is 50%, the concentration of Cu-NCDs is defined as half inhibition concentration (IC50). It represents the inhibition capability of CDs quantificationally. Clearly the smaller IC50 is, the more toxic the CDs is. Herein the IC50 was calculated by SPSS 23.0 package. The HepG2 cells grew well after adding 200 μL of Cu-NCDs with the concentration of 200 μg mL−1, seen from Fig. S2.

The as-prepared Cu-NCDs were used for HepG2 cell imaging. The HepG2 cells were cultured in 96-well plates first for 24 h in an incubator (37 °C, 5% CO2), and for another 24 h after the culture medium was replaced by 200 μL of fresh DMEM, containing the Cu-NCDs with the concentration of 100 μg mL−1 with a further 6 h of incubation. The excess Cu-NCDs were removed by washing 2 times with PBS and then fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde. And the in fluorescence imaging pictures were taken at a laser scanning confocal microscope (Nano Focus, GER).

The TEM images (Fig. 2 a) indicated that the average particle diameter of Cu-NCDs were 3.57 nm. The HRTEM images (Fig. 2a, inset) revealed that the Cu-NCDs were crystalline with a lattice-spacing distance of around 0.21 nm, which corresponded to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon [34,35], and it was similar to previous reports that used FA as a raw material [23,36]. The XRD pattern of the Cu-NCDs was shown in Fig. 2b Two broad diffraction peaks were observed at 23.56° (d002 = 0.34 nm) and 43.86° (d100 = 0.21 nm), which corresponds to the amorphous carbon and graphitization carbon. This originated from the abundant surface functional groups and graphitization carbon, respectively [37]. Furthermore, the diffraction peaks arising from Cu also appeared in XRD, which demonstrated that Cu-coordinating and/or doping structures existing in Cu-NCDs.

The FT-IR spectra of NCDs and Cu-NCDs were displayed in Fig. 3 . The detailed analysis of FT-IR spectrum for NCDs was in the supporting information. For Cu-NCDs, the characteristic peaks at 3455, 3350 and 3175 cm−1 corresponded to stretching vibrations of O/N–H. Characteristic peaks at about 3045, 2980 and 2920 cm−1 were assigned to the C–H alkyl stretching vibrations, and the C–H alkyl bending vibration also appeared at 1404 cm−1. The peaks at around 1735, 1690 and 1500 cm−1 were related to a stretching vibration of –COOH, CO and CN, respectively. The NCDs and Cu-NCDs both possessed absorption bands at 1540 and 1443 cm−1, which were related to the vibration of C = C in the benzene ring. This indicated the presence of aromatic structure in the CDs and the formation of an unsaturated CC bond in the carbon core, which was consistent with the HRTEM and XRD results. The peaks at 1378, 1325 and 1295 cm−1 were attributed to the aromatic heterocyclic C–N–C, N-(C)3 and C–N–C stretching vibrations, respectively. The asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of C–O–C in the range of 1217–1000 cm−1 were observed. In addition, the bending vibration of N–H also appeared at 837 cm−1. The abundant amino and carboxyl groups on the CD surface were beneficial for chelation with Cu ions, which then promoted the Cu doping and surface passivation process. Unlike NCDs, a series of metal-sensitive bonds appeared in the range of 870–1000 cm−1 for Cu-NCDs, which could be ascribed to N–Cu–N [26]. The results from FT-IR indicated that N and Cu containing groups have formed on the surface and framework of Cu-NCD. The apparent zeta potential (ζ) for Cu-NCDs was +5.33 mV, approximately (Fig. S2a). This demonstrated the existence of charged component positively, for instance, amide and Cu-containing groups on the surface of Cu-NCDs, which obviously improved the interaction between Cu-NCDs and analytes.

To further confirm the structure information of Cu-CNDs, XPS measurements were undertaken. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the XPS full-scan spectrum showed four obvious peaks attributed to C, N, O and Cu, and the element contents were 49.84%, 21.34%, 27.47% and 1.35%, respectively. The high content of Cu confirmed that Cu was successfully doped in the CDs. In the high-resolution spectra (Fig. 4 b–d), the C1s band could be deconvoluted into five peaks at 284.10, 285.35, 286.10, 287.50 and 288.35 eV, which corresponded to CC/C–C, C–N/O, CN, CO and OC–O/N, respectively; the N1s band could be deconvoluted into two peaks at 398.85 and 399.95 eV, which corresponded to sp [2]-hybridized CN, porphyrin C–N–C and amino N in the form of N-(C)3 or H–N-(C)2, respectively. Moreover, the Cu2p spectrum exhibited two predominant peaks at 934.45 and 954.20 eV, which corresponded to the Cu2p2/3 and Cu2p1/2 spin-orbit peaks of Cu, respectively [38,39]. In addition, four satellite peaks, observed at 932.20, 941.40, 943.80 and 952.15 eV, were the characteristic peaks of the divalent copper ion phase. The presence of a divalent copper ion imparted a great chelation ability for small molecules.

The UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra for NCDs and Cu-NCDs were shown in Fig. S3. They all processed conspicuous absorption bands at ~276 and ~340 nm, which originated from the π-π* transition of CC and n-π* transition of C＝O/N of CDs, respectively [40]. Nevertheless, the absorption band for the n-π* transition of C＝O/N in Cu-NCDs was more obvious and stronger than that in NCDs, which originated from the transition to metal Cu doping. In addition, benefitting from Cu doping and bonding, the UV–Vis absorption of Cu-NCDs had a wider absorption band at 300–600 nm compared with the absorption band of NCDs at 300–400 nm [41]. Interestingly, the Cu-NCDs showed dual fluorescence emission peaks at 410 and 470 nm, which was distinct from NCDs that emitted fluorescence at 450 nm. This demonstrated that the carbon core structure and surface state of the CDs may already have been changed via doped metal Cu, which led to the fluorescence emission center changing and the a new emission center appearing. This further confirmed that the Cu-NCDs could be synthesized legitimately by current method.

The Cu-NCD solution showed blue luminescence under 365 nm excitation, as shown in Fig. 5 (a, inset). The photoluminescence excitation spectrum (PLE) tested at the emission wavelength of 470 nm showed three peaks at 276, 340 and 365 nm, which were consistent with the UV–Vis absorption result, seen from Fig. 5a [42]. To explore the fluorescence origin for Cu-NCDs, the fluorescence emission spectra for Cu-NCDs excited at different wavelength were tested. Obviously, when the excitation wavelength varied between 280 and 300 nm (π-π*) and 320–400 nm (n-π*), there was only a change in the fluorescence intensity but no shift in the peak position, and the three main positions at 360, 410 and 470 nm were unchanged, as observed from Fig. 5b. However, the red-shift phenomenon occurred with the excitation wavelength increasing more than 420 nm gradually. As the excitation wavelength was at about 340 nm, the strongest fluorescence emission peaks appeared at around 470 nm. The excitation-independent fluorescence emission may originate from the π-π* and n-π* transitions. The excitation-dependent fluorescence emission may benefit from the abundance of surface states, and indicated the multicolor properties of the Cu-NCDs [43]. The fluorescence emission spectra for NCDs excited at different wavelengths were also obtained, seen from Fig. S4. The NCDs showed a similar fluorescence emission behavior to Cu-NCDs. Excitation-independent fluorescence emission was recorded in the excitation wavelengths range of 320–380 nm. There was only a change in the fluorescence intensity but no shift in the peak positions, and the two main positions at 355 and 450 nm remained unchanged, and the maximum fluorescence emission intensity was examined via exciting at 360 nm. Similarly, the emission peak was also red-shifting while the excitation wavelength surpassed 400 nm, which demonstrated the excitation-dependent emission property.

Fig. 5c is the fluorescence emission of the Cu-NCDs under different pH environment. 3 mol L−1 of HCl and 1 mol L−1 of NaOH were used to regulating the pH of the Cu-NCD solution. The fluorescence emission peak at 410 nm remained unchanged in both the strongly acidic and alkaline conditions. It is reasonable to assume that the fluorescence emission center may arise from the stable Cu doping. The fluorescence emission intensity at 470 nm increased from pH = 1 to the maximum value at pH = 11, but decreased from pH = 11 to 14. In addition, the maximum fluorescence emission gradually red-shifted from 470 to 495 nm from pH = 5 to 1, which revealed that the low pH can destroy the fluorescence emission sites of the Cu-NCDs. The pH-dependent fluorescence emission property for the peak at 470 nm demonstrated a fluorescence emission origin from the surface functional groups. Different from previous reports [36,44], the fluorescence intensity increased as the pH increasing. The deprotonation/protonation of the amide and carboxyl groups of the Cu-NCDs, which causes electrostatic doping/charging of the Cu-NCDs and a shift of the Fermi level, was known as the main reason for an optical response to pH [45]. An excessive pH value can increase the number of negative charges on the surface of the Cu-NCDs, which influences the n-π* transitions to lead to the fluorescence intensity decreasing. The Cu-NCDs could be exploited as a pH indicator owing to its highly sensitive response to pH.

N and Cu doping was a critical reason for improving the analytical detection ability of CDs. Because N and Cu can bring about positive surface charge, which obviously improved the interaction between CDs and analyte [24]. Moreover, it was easy for Cu to chelate with most small molecules, which improved the selectivity and sensitivity for CDs towards the analyte.

The fluorescence emission of the Cu-NCDs can be quenched by AA in pH = 7.4 PBS solution. Fig. 6 a-b indicated that the fluorescence intensity of the Cu-NCDs at 470 nm gradually decreased with AA concentration increasing. Figure (6 a, inset) showed a good linear correlation (R2 = 0.9958) for fluorescence intensity by AA concentration in the range of 0.02–40 μM, giving a linear regression equation of F = 762.84–0.842 [AA concentration] (R2 = 0.992). The limit of detection (LOD) is estimated to be 17.8 nM at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3.

The fluorescence response ability of Cu-NCDs towards metal ions and other organic small molecules was explored to investigate the selectivity for AA. The Cu-NCDs possessed great selectivity, and the fluorescence quenching effect was strongest in the presence of AA, seen from Fig. 6 c-d. Moreover, the fluorescence spectrum of NCDs in the presence of AA with a concentration of 1000 μM was also explored under the same conditions in Fig. S5. It was found that there was no obvious change in fluorescence intensity, which indicated that the active Cu functional groups contained in CDs played a significant role in AA determination.

The AA determination mechanism was investigated. Two typical mechanisms of fluorescence quenching were static or dynamic quenching. Dynamic quenching was associated with diffusion related interplay between quenchers and fluorescent substance. Static quenching was associated with the conformation of the complex between the quencher and the fluorescence molecules. Usually, Stern–Volmer equations were used to explore static and dynamic quenching.
Dynamic quenching, F0/F = KSV [Q] + 1.
Static quenching, F0/F = Kq [Q] +1.

The F0 and F were the fluorescence intensities at 470 nm in the absence and presence of AA, respectively, [Q] is the AA concentration and KSV is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant. As shown the inset of Fig. 6b, a linear regression equation of F0/F = 1.02 + 0.0012 × [AA concentration] in the range of 0.02–40 μM (R2 = 0.993) was obtained. The fluorescence lifetime for Cu-NCDs was measured to be 6.68 ns, in Fig. 7 a. The formula Kqτ0 = Ksv was usually applied for computing the bimolecular annihilation constant Kq. A typical quenching constant for fluorescent substance is 1.0–2.0 × 1010 L mol−1 s−1. Hence, the bimolecular annihilation constant was 1.8 × 1011 L mol−1 s−1, which was larger than a typical quenching constant. This indicated that the fluorescence quenching was SQE. Furthermore, UV–Vis absorption spectra of the Cu-NCDs were measured in the presence of AA in Fig. 7b. The absorption band of the Cu-NCDs at 276 nm disappeared after adding AA, but a new absorption band arose at 246 nm. This indicated the formation of new non-luminescent complex based on a chelation possess between Cu and multiple hydroxyls in AA, which further stood by the SQE mechanism. In addition, the zeta potential value of Cu-NCDs also changed from +5.33 to −17.1 mV after adding AA, as shown in Fig. S6 a, a’, which demonstrated that AA was selectively coordinated with the CDs leading to fluorescence quenching of Cu-NCDs. The heteroatom doping played a significant role in the AA determination process. On the one hand, Cu doping can increase positively charged surface of CDs (zeta potential value of +0.75 mV and +5.33 mV for NCDs and Cu-NCDs, respectively), as shown in Fig. S6 a, b, which improved the interaction towards AA. On the other hand, the effective chelation effect of Cu towards multiple hydroxyls in AA can also improve the selectivity and sensitivity. Then, stable Cu-NCD-based non-luminescent compounds were finally formed, which resulted in SQE.

There were many advantages compared with the previous fluorescent probes used, as shown in Table S1. At present, most of reported CDs-based AA probes were indirect detection process (by redox reaction) and originated from dynamic quenching mechanism, they usually need to introduce other oxidants such as those of metal ions Cr6+ 39, Cu2+ 40 and Fe3+ 41 for auxiliary detection. Current method simplifies the operation and reduces the interference from impurities. As we known, static quenching was associated with the conformation of the complex between the quencher and the fluorescence molecules. Therefore, the static quenching mechanism was beneficial to explore the surface state of CDs and further facilitate the exploration of the fluorescence origin of CDs [31–33,46–53]. In addition, the Cu-NCDs needn't passivating by organic molecules or compositing with other materials, which can provide more suitable linear determination range and lower LOD than other CD-based probes for detecting AA47, 52, 61. What's more, the heavy metal quantum dots [48,49] and rare earth materials [53,54] were potentially harmful to humans and the environment, while noble metal nanoclusters [50,51] were relatively expensive compared with a CD-based AA probes.

Bioimaging is one of the most significant applications for CDs benefiting from their excellent fluorescence property and high biocompatibility. However, some metal-doped CDs are noxious to cells and organism in high concentrations, therefore, the development for metal-doped CDs with low cytotoxicity is necessary.

The IC50 was calculated to be high to 812.96 μg mL−1, indicating that the Cu-NCDs exhibited low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility. The fluorescence images of HepG2 cells were obtained after incubating with 100 μg mL−1 of Cu-NCDs for 6 h at 37 °C by different light excitations in Fig. 8 B. The results showed that the Cu-NCDs labeled HepG2 cells can be used for multicolor cellular imaging. Moreover, it was found that the luminescence from Cu-NCDs mainly at the cytoplasm instead of the cell nucleus. This demonstrated that Cu-NCDs could cross the cell membrane barriers into the intracellular region, but can't enter the cellular nuclei.

Cu-NCDs with dual fluorescence emission were prepared by solvothermal approach. The N-containing groups including sp2-hybridized CN, porphyrin C–N–C and amino N in N-(C) 3 or H–N-(C) 2, and the Cu-containing group, N–Cu–N, have formed on the surface and framework of Cu-NCD. The element doping and surface functionalization of Cu-NCDs endowed their novel chemical, optical and electrical properties. The Cu-NCDs displayed excitation wavelength dependent and pH-dependent fluorescence property. Benefitted by the rewarding brought by chelation possess between Cu and multi-hydroxyl in AA and N and Cu doping causing positively charged surface of CDs. The Cu-NCDs were used as an effective fluorescent probe for AA concentrations of 0.02–40 μM with an LOD of 17.8 nM. And the mechanism of the quenching procedure is a non-oxidation reduction strategy based on SQE. Finally, the as-prepared Cu-NCDs are successfully used as HepG2 cell imaging agent.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 31890773, 31570567), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (Nos. 2572018AB02).

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2019.120649.
